content:
How AI Cost Firewall evolved from a token-saving gateway into a control plane for LLM cost management, privacy, security, audit, and compliance.

5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

VCAL Privacy, Security, Audit, and Governance modulesWhen I began working with AI chatbots and agentic workflows, token usage quickly became one of the most practical engineering problems.
It was not only about large prompts. Repeated questions, similar requests, long conversation histories, retries, and multi-step agent flows all increased the number of calls to cloud AI providers. The application could work well technically, while the cost profile remained difficult to control.
That was the starting point for my work on AI cost optimization.

The first step: semantic caching
My initial approach was a lightweight semantic cache server.
The server checks whether a similar request has already been processed. If a reliable match is found, it can return the existing response instead of creating a new upstream LLM call. This reduces repeated token usage, improves response time, and gives teams better control over the economics of their AI applications.
That work became VCAL Semantic Cache Server.
I wrote about the first version of this idea here.
The solution proved that semantic caching can be more than a small optimization. It can become a practical infrastructure layer for companies that need durable, observable, and deployable cost control for AI systems.
At the same time, I saw another side of the problem.
A production-grade semantic cache server requires careful configuration. Teams need to understand embeddings, similarity thresholds, index behavior, storage, expiration, observability, and deployment topology. For engineering teams that want deep control, this is valuable. But many teams first need a simpler entry point.
That led to AI Cost Firewall.

From semantic cache to AI Cost Firewall
AI Cost Firewall uses the same core concept of reusing previous responses where it is safe and useful to do so.
The difference is the deployment model.
AI Cost Firewall works as an OpenAI-compatible gateway between an application and an upstream LLM provider. Applications call AI Cost Firewall instead of calling the provider directly.
Behind the scenes, AI Cost Firewall can use:

• Redis for exact request-response reuse.

• Qdrant for semantically similar request-response reuse.

• Prometheus and Grafana for operational visibility.
This makes the first deployment easier.
A team can place AI Cost Firewall in front of an existing LLM application, watch cache hit rates, measure upstream request reduction, and see the savings in built-in Grafana dashboards.
Cost control becomes visible, not theoretical.

Cost was only the beginning
While developing AI Cost Firewall, I started to see a broader role for the gateway.
If every prompt and response already passes through this layer, the same control point can support more than caching. It can also enforce privacy rules, apply security checks, create audit trails, and support compliance workflows.
That changed the direction of the project.
The name stays the same: AI Cost Firewall.
But the platform is expanding with additional VCAL modules:

• VCAL Privacy Guard

• VCAL Security Guard

• VCAL Audit

• VCAL Compliance
Together, these modules turn AI Cost Firewall from a cost-saving gateway into a broader security and governance control plane for LLM infrastructure.

VCAL Privacy Guard
VCAL Privacy Guard focuses on detecting and protecting sensitive information before it reaches an LLM provider.
This can include emails, IP addresses, phone numbers, API keys, bearer tokens, JWTs, private keys, and other sensitive values. Depending on the deployment mode, the module can detect, redact, or anonymize sensitive content.
For example, instead of sending this upstream:
Analyze login from 185.23.10.5 by john@example.comThe system can send:
Analyze login from [IP_1] by [EMAIL_1]After the response is returned, the platform can restore the original values where appropriate.
This helps organizations reduce exposure of sensitive data while keeping the business value of the LLM workflow.

VCAL Security Guard
VCAL Security Guard is intended to focus on security checks around LLM traffic.
This includes suspicious inputs, unsafe instructions, prompt injection attempts, malicious patterns, and policy violations that may require blocking, warning, or escalation.
This becomes especially important with agentic systems.
Unlike simple chatbots, agentic workflows can call tools, retrieve documents, interact with APIs, and trigger business processes. That makes runtime control more important than in traditional request-response applications.
Security checks should be part of the AI infrastructure path, not an afterthought.

VCAL Audit and VCAL Compliance
VCAL Audit focuses on traceability.
For enterprise environments, it is not enough to know that an AI system produced a response. Teams often need to understand what happened, when it happened, what policy was applied, what data was detected, which request was cached, which request was sent upstream, and which control decisions were made.
VCAL Compliance builds on this by helping turn technical controls into compliance-ready evidence.
The goal is not to replace legal, compliance, or governance teams. The goal is to give them structured records, policy evidence, and predictable reporting based on what the AI infrastructure actually did.

A flexible platform architecture
AI Cost Firewall can start with Redis and Qdrant. This keeps deployment simple and makes the first cost-saving results easy to measure.
For larger production deployments, Redis and Qdrant can be replaced or complemented by VCAL Semantic Cache Server. In that setup, the platform becomes more durable, more controlled, and better suited for enterprise AI infrastructure.
This creates a natural path:
1. Start with AI Cost Firewall.
2. Measure cost savings and cache hit rates.
3. Add privacy and security controls.
4. Add audit and compliance features.
5. Move to VCAL Semantic Cache Server when deeper production control is required.
At that point, the system is no longer just a caching gateway.
It becomes a cost, privacy, security, audit, and compliance control plane for LLM applications.

Integration with SIEM and SOAR
The output produced by AI Cost Firewall and its VCAL modules can also be sent to SIEM or SOAR systems.
This allows AI-related events to become part of the existing security monitoring and incident response process.
A SIEM can receive events about sensitive data detection, anonymized prompts, blocked requests, suspicious prompt patterns, upstream provider usage, cache bypass events, policy decisions, and audit records.
A SOAR system can use those events to trigger workflows, open tickets, notify teams, or start additional investigation.
AI systems are becoming part of business-critical processes. They should be monitored and governed with the same seriousness as other enterprise systems.

From cost visibility to governance
The journey started with a practical problem: reducing token spend.
But solving that problem created a useful architectural position. Once AI Cost Firewall became the gateway for LLM traffic, it became clear that the same layer could support much more than cost optimization.
Cost control was the entry point.
Privacy, security, auditability, and compliance are the next steps.
AI Cost Firewall started as a way to reduce repeated LLM calls. It is now becoming a foundation for a secure and auditable AI platform.
For companies moving from AI experiments to production deployments, that difference matters.
Originally published on the VCAL Labs blog
VCAL Labs builds lightweight Rust infrastructure for AI systems, including semantic caching and AI traffic control layers.
https://vcal-project.com/
